text.title: Docker Compose Patterns: NERO Infrastructure Stack
text.summary: NERO-style infrastructure stack (postgres + redis + rabbitmq), profile patterns for optional services, and environment variable precedence.

section.title: Infrastructure Stack Design
section.p: Run stateful infrastructure (PostgreSQL, Redis, RabbitMQ) in Docker Compose. Run application code (Python/Node services) as systemd user services. This gives reproducible infrastructure setup without container overhead in the application hot path.

rule.statement: Do not run Python or Node.js application services in Docker Compose on a VPS where they are deployed via rsync. The rsync/systemd pattern is simpler and faster; Docker adds a layer of indirection with no benefit.
rule.rationale: rsync deploys run in milliseconds and require no image builds. systemd user services restart on crash, log to journald, and have resource limits. Adding Docker to this stack for application code requires Dockerfile maintenance, image builds, and volume mounts — all overhead without a corresponding benefit on a single-server setup.

rule.statement: Assign optional monitoring and admin tools to named profiles (`monitoring`, `dev`). They do not start with `docker compose up -d` but are available on demand with `--profile monitoring`.
rule.rationale: Flower (Celery monitor), pgAdmin, and Prometheus are useful for debugging but consume RAM constantly when running. Profiles let you activate them for a session without permanently altering the production stack.

example.code:
services: # Always runs postgres: image: postgres:16-alpine
# Only with: docker compose --profile monitoring up -d flower: image: mher/flower profiles: ["monitoring"] ports: ["127.0.0.1:5555:5555"]
pgadmin: image: dpage/pgadmin4 profiles: ["dev"] ports: ["127.0.0.1:5050:80"]
docker compose up -d # postgres + redis + rabbitmq only docker compose --profile monitoring up -d # + flower docker compose --profile monitoring --profile dev up -d # + flower + pgadmin

rule.statement: Use a single `.env` file next to `docker-compose.yml` for Compose variable substitution. For secrets passed into containers, use `env_file:` pointing to the same file. Shell environment variables take precedence over `.env`.
rule.rationale: Compose reads `.env` automatically for `${VARIABLE}` substitution in the YAML. Containers only see variables passed via `environment:` or `env_file:`. Keeping both in the same file avoids duplication and ensures the container environment matches the Compose interpolation context.

example.code: Precedence (highest to lowest): 1. Shell environment variables (set before running docker compose) 2. .env file (Compose interpolation) (project_dir/.env) 3. env_file: directive (per-service secrets file) 4. environment: directive defaults (inline in YAML)

rule.statement: Use `restart: unless-stopped` on all infrastructure services. This restarts on crash and after host reboot, but respects an explicit `docker compose down` (useful for maintenance).
rule.rationale: `restart: always` would restart a container you explicitly stopped with `docker stop` or `docker compose down`. `unless-stopped` is the correct production policy: restart on crash and reboot, but respect intentional stops.

section.title: Connecting Systemd Services to Docker Infrastructure
section.p: Application services running as systemd user services connect to Docker containers via `localhost` and the bound port.

example.code:
# ~/workspace/.env (shared by both Docker Compose and systemd services) DATABASE_URL=postgresql://nero:${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}@127.0.0.1:5432/nero_db REDIS_URL=redis://127.0.0.1:6379/0 RABBITMQ_URL=amqp://nero:${RABBITMQ_PASSWORD}@127.0.0.1:5672/nero
# Compose binds ports to 127.0.0.1:5432, 127.0.0.1:6379, 127.0.0.1:5672 # Systemd services load .env and connect via localhost

see-also.link: Full postgres + redis + rabbitmq stack with healthchecks
Core rules: UFW bypass, healthchecks, volumes
